comments.body: What do you mean by "browserless"? In case you mean "headless": Yes, you also need to download the browsers when running in headless mode.
Delegating the call to a external provider Browserless is one of them.
But i want to do it programmatically i mean depending of the configuration sometimes it will run in the browsers in the same machine and sometimes in different providers.
Sorry, missed that bit. How to set an env var programmatically in Java depends on your OS, lots of possibilities covered here: stackoverflow.com/questions/318239/…

answers.body:
You can skip the browser download by setting an environment variable PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD=1

See the docs here: https://playwright.dev/java/docs/browsers#skip-browser-downloads

Map<String,String> env = new HashMap<>();
env.put("PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD","1");
try (Playwright playwright = Playwright.create(new Playwright.CreateOptions().setEnv(env))) {
        BrowserType chromium = playwright.chromium();
        Browser browser = chromium.launch();
        Page page = browser.newPage();
        page.onRequest(request -> System.out.println(">> " + request.method() + " " + request.url()));
        page.onResponse(response -> System.out.println("<<" + response.status() + " " + response.url()));
        page.navigate("https://example.com");
        browser.close();
    }
}

you can decide if you want to use the browser in headed mode or not.
You can see example in https://github.com/risheeboy/grj to figure out how to run the same tests in both modes, in a Java based project.

app/src/test/java/org/example/PlaywrightTest.java

package org.example;

import com.microsoft.playwright.*;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.*;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

public class PlaywrightTest {
    @Test
    void shouldShowPlaywrightHome() {
        try (Playwright playwright = Playwright.create()) {
            Browser browser = playwright.chromium().launch(new BrowserType.LaunchOptions()
                    .setHeadless(!Boolean.getBoolean("headed")));
            Page page = browser.newPage();
            page.navigate("https://playwright.dev");
            page.waitForTimeout(2000);
            assertTrue(page.title().contains("Playwright"));
        }
    }
}

app/build.gradle:

plugins {
    id 'java'
}

repositories {
    mavenCentral()
}

dependencies {
    testImplementation platform('org.junit:junit-bom:5.10.0')
    testImplementation 'org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter'
    testImplementation 'com.microsoft.playwright:playwright:1.56.0'
}

test {
    useJUnitPlatform()
    systemProperty 'headed', System.getProperty('headed')
}

task codegen(type: JavaExec) {
    classpath = sourceSets.test.runtimeClasspath
    mainClass = 'com.microsoft.playwright.CLI'
    // Default args
    args = ['codegen']
    doFirst {
        // --args overwrites the default args, so we need to ensure 'codegen' is still there
        if (args.isEmpty() || args[0] != 'codegen') {
            args = ['codegen'] + args
        }
        println "Executing Playwright CLI with args: ${args}"
    }
}

settings.gradle:

rootProject.name = 'playwright-demo'
include('app')

README.md

# Playwright Java Project

This is a minimal Java project using Gradle to run Playwright tests.

## Prerequisites

- Java 17 or higher (Java 21 recommended)

## Setup

### Mac / Linux
If you have the project for the first time, you can build it using the Gradle Wrapper:

```bash
./gradlew build

If gradlew is missing or not executable, you can bootstrap it (if the script is provided) or ensure you have Gradle installed.

Windows

gradlew.bat build

Running Tests

To run the Playwright tests:

Mac / Linux

./gradlew test

Windows

gradlew.bat test

Headed Mode

To run the tests in headed mode (browser visible):

./gradlew test -Dheaded=true

Code Generation

You can use Playwright's code generator to record user interactions and generate Java code.

To launch the codegen tool:

./gradlew codegen

To generate code for a specific website (e.g., Google):

./gradlew codegen --args="https://www.google.com"

title: Java Playwright + Browserless ignore browser download
body:
I am developing a simple prototype with Java + Playwright + Browserless. I was wondering if it's possible to avoid downloading the browsers in AWS.

Playwright build of chromium v956323 downloaded to /home/ec2-user/.cache/ms-playwright/chromium-956323
Playwright build of ffmpeg v1007 downloaded to /home/ec2-user/.cache/ms-playwright/ffmpeg-1007
Playwright build of firefox v1313 downloaded to /home/ec2-user/.cache/ms-playwright/firefox-1313
Playwright build of webkit v1596 downloaded to /home/ec2-user/.cache/ms-playwright/webkit-1596

Sometimes it will run in browserless and I think if so I won't need the browser but I don't really know is this possible. Or even running in browserless would I need the browsers?

If so, how can I do that in Java?

I want to do it programmatically because depending of the parameters sometime I need them, sometimes I don't; is this possible?
